About.label: About
About.title: About MAB SERVICES
About.p1: MAB SERVICES, represented by Mohammed Amine BENZAKOUR, life insurance broker and financial security advisor, provides services in Ontario and Québec.
About.p2: He guides clients in finding solutions tailored to their personal and family needs, particularly in life insurance and in savings and investment. Savings strategies are implemented through registered plans, non-registered plans, or life insurance solutions.
About.p3: An approach aimed at protecting loved ones, preparing for the future and meeting various financial security objectives, while offering a simple, transparent and personalized process that allows each client to choose the solutions best suited to their situation.

Insurance.label: Insurance
Insurance.title: INSURANCE AND PROTECTION SOLUTIONS
Insurance.intro: We offer a comprehensive range of coverages to secure your assets and protect your loved ones from the unexpected.

term.title: Term Life Insurance
term.desc: Flexible protection to cover your financial needs over a defined period. Ideal for protecting a mortgage, debts or dependants during critical years.

perm.title: Permanent Life Insurance
perm.desc: Guaranteed lifetime coverage, often including a savings component or cash value. An effective estate planning tool.

disability.title: Disability Insurance
disability.desc: Essential income replacement if you become unable to work due to an accident or illness. Protect your lifestyle and your family's.

critical.title: Critical Illness Insurance
critical.desc: A tax-free lump-sum payment to support you financially upon diagnosis of a covered critical illness (cancer, heart attack, stroke, etc.).

health.title: Health and Dental Insurance
health.desc: Supplemental coverage for medical, dental, vision and paramedical expenses not covered by provincial health plans. Ideal for self-employed individuals, retirees and employees without group benefits.

travel.title: Travel and Emergency Medical Insurance
travel.desc: Essential protection when travelling outside Canada or your province. Covers emergency medical expenses, hospitalization and medical evacuation, along with other benefits depending on the plan selected.

faq.q: What is the difference between term and permanent life insurance?
faq.a: Term life insurance covers a fixed period — 10, 20 or 30 years. It is ideal for protecting a mortgage, debts or dependants during critical years. Permanent life insurance (whole life or universal life) covers you for your entire lifetime and can accumulate a cash value accessible during your lifetime, making it a powerful estate planning tool. The right choice depends on your time horizon, your budget and your wealth transfer goals.

faq.q: Do I need disability insurance if my employer provides it?
faq.a: This coverage ends if you change jobs, become self-employed or take early retirement. A personal disability policy, funded by your own premiums, pays tax-free benefits and follows you regardless of your employment status. According to the Canadian Life and Health Insurance Association, 1 in 3 Canadians will be disabled for more than 90 days before age 65 — a risk very few households can absorb without adequate protection.

faq.q: Is critical illness insurance really necessary?
faq.a: The cost depends on your age, health status, lifestyle habits (such as smoking) and the coverage amount you choose. Securing coverage while young and in good health allows you to lock in a favourable rate for the long term. Comparing quotes from multiple insurers is essential to get the best value for your needs.

faq.q: Can I get coverage if I have a pre-existing condition?
faq.a: Available options vary depending on your health situation and may include a standard policy with an exclusion for the specific condition, a policy with a higher premium, or a no-medical guaranteed-issue policy. Conditions such as controlled diabetes, treated hypertension, a history of depression or excess weight can often be accepted, with adjustments as needed. Since insurers do not all assess risk the same way, decisions may vary from one company to another. It is important to provide complete and accurate information at the time of application, as any omission or inaccuracy may affect the validity of the contract or the settlement of a claim.

faq.q: When should I review my insurance coverage?
faq.a: Certain life events should prompt a review of your situation, including marriage, common-law partnership, the birth or adoption of a child, the purchase of a property, a job change or loss, a significant increase in income, a divorce or the death of a spouse. Beyond these events, a periodic review is recommended, as personal and financial situations evolve over time. As your mortgage decreases, children grow up and wealth accumulates, coverage adjustments may be needed. Regular reviews help maintain appropriate protection and avoid being either under-insured or over-insured.

Savings.label: Savings
Savings.title: SAVINGS AND INVESTMENT PLANS
Savings.intro: Discover the different investment vehicles to optimize your taxes and reach your financial goals.

celi.title: TFSA — Tax-Free Savings Account
celi.desc: A plan that lets you set money aside sheltered from tax. Investment gains (interest, dividends, capital gains) earned in a TFSA are not taxable, even when withdrawn.

celiapp.title: FHSA — First Home Savings Account
celiapp.desc: A plan designed to help individuals save for the purchase of their first home. It combines the benefits of an RRSP (tax-deductible contributions) and a TFSA (tax-free withdrawals for a home purchase).

reer.title: RRSP — Registered Retirement Savings Plan
reer.desc: A plan designed primarily for retirement savings. Contributions are deductible from your taxable income, which reduces your annual tax payable. Tax is deferred until funds are withdrawn.

reee.title: RESP — Registered Education Savings Plan
reee.desc: A special investment account used by parents to save for a child's post-secondary education. This plan gives access to generous government grants added to your savings.

reei.title: RDSP — Registered Disability Savings Plan
reei.desc: A savings plan that helps Canadians living with a disability and their families save to ensure their long-term financial security. It also allows access to government grants and bonds.

faq.q: What is the difference between an RRSP and a TFSA?
faq.a: The RRSP (Registered Retirement Savings Plan) lets you deduct contributions from your taxable income, reducing your tax bill immediately. Growth is tax-deferred, but withdrawals are added to your taxable income in retirement. The TFSA (Tax-Free Savings Account) offers no upfront tax deduction, but all withdrawals — including gains — are completely tax-free. Strategy: favour the RRSP if your current marginal tax rate is high, the TFSA if your income is lower or if you want complete flexibility without tax consequences on withdrawal.

faq.q: What is the FHSA and who can use it?
faq.a: The FHSA (First Home Savings Account), launched in 2023, is designed exclusively for first-time home buyers. It combines the benefits of an RRSP (tax-deductible contributions) and a TFSA (qualifying withdrawals for a home purchase are completely tax-free). Unused contribution room carries forward to the following year. To be eligible, you must not have owned your principal residence in recent years. It can be combined with the RRSP Home Buyers' Plan (HBP) to maximize your down payment.

faq.q: How does the Canada Education Savings Grant (CESG) work in an RESP?
faq.a: The federal government automatically contributes a percentage-based grant on annual RESP contributions. Families with low or middle incomes may receive an additional grant. Québec also offers the Quebec Education Savings Incentive (QESI). Unused CESG room from previous years can be carried forward and caught up in future years. When funds are withdrawn for education, they are taxed in the student's hands — typically at a very low rate given their modest income during studies.

faq.q: Can I withdraw from my RRSP before retirement?
faq.a: Yes, but important tax consequences apply. Outside of specific programs, any RRSP withdrawal is added to your taxable income for the year and is subject to a progressive withholding tax. Two programs allow tax-deferred withdrawals: the Home Buyers' Plan (HBP), for a first home purchase, and the Lifelong Learning Plan (LLP), for full-time education. Unlike the TFSA, regular RRSP withdrawals do not restore contribution room — that room is permanently lost.

faq.q: At what age should I start investing?
faq.a: As early as possible. Compound growth is the most powerful force in personal finance: even small, regular contributions invested early can make a significant difference over the long term. The priority at any age: first capture any employer matching contributions if available, then maximize the FHSA if you plan to buy a home, then split between RRSP and TFSA based on your tax situation. A free consultation with MAB Services helps establish a realistic, personalized plan starting today.

faq.q: Should I pay off my debt or invest in a TFSA or RRSP?
faq.a: The general rule: pay off high-interest debt first — that is a guaranteed return that few investments consistently beat. Credit card debt and high-interest personal loans are always the priority. For a low-rate mortgage, investing in parallel often makes more financial sense, especially in an RRSP if the tax refund is reinvested. If your employer offers RRSP matching, always contribute enough to capture the full match first. The optimal balance depends on your marginal tax rate, time horizon and risk tolerance.

Privacy.label: Legal
Privacy.title: Privacy Policy
Privacy.body: Personal information collected through this site is processed in accordance with applicable privacy laws. The information provided is used solely for the purpose of delivering our services, analyzing insurance needs, communicating with clients and prospects, and managing requests. Reasonable security measures are in place to protect personal information against unauthorized access or disclosure, subject to applicable legal and contractual obligations.
